# The Finite Difference Method of Discretization in CFD

### Key Takeaways

The numerical method of solving differential equations by approximating them with difference equations is called the finite difference method.

The finite difference method can easily obtain high-order approximations.

The finite difference method requires a structured grid.

*![The finite difference method in CFD](https://content.cdntwrk.com/files/aHViPTExODYyNSZjbWQ9aXRlbWVkaXRvcmltYWdlJmZpbGVuYW1lPWl0ZW1lZGl0b3JpbWFnZV82MzUwNzU3MWViMmU5LmpwZyZ2ZXJzaW9uPTAwMDAmc2lnPTkwZGZkMzhkZGYxMmU4OGFlYTljNGM4NDkxOGQ5Yzky)*

![The finite difference method in CFD](https://content.cdntwrk.com/files/aHViPTExODYyNSZjbWQ9aXRlbWVkaXRvcmltYWdlJmZpbGVuYW1lPWl0ZW1lZGl0b3JpbWFnZV82MzUwNzU3MWViMmU5LmpwZyZ2ZXJzaW9uPTAwMDAmc2lnPTkwZGZkMzhkZGYxMmU4OGFlYTljNGM4NDkxOGQ5Yzky)

*Different discretization methods such as the finite element method, finite volume method, and finite difference method are used in CFD modeling*

In engineering problems, we often come across linear and non-linear differential equations. These governing equations must be solved, and the analytical method of solving them can not be used due to their complexity. However, it is possible to use computational fluid dynamic techniques to obtain computer-based solutions for these complex equations. Different discretization methods, such as the finite element method, finite volume method, and finite difference method, can be used in the CFD modeling of these engineering problems. In this article, we will explore the finite difference method of discretization in detail.

## Solving Complex Equations With CFD

Laws of physics are associated with various physical phenomena like [heat transfer](https://resources.system-analysis.cadence.com/blog/msa2022-explaining-the-finite-difference-method-and-heat-transfer) and fluid flow. They need to be represented as mathematical equations to simulate these phenomena over various length scales. Take fluid dynamics, for example: governing equations are obtained by applying the fundamental laws of mechanics to the fluid. The conservation of mass equation, the conservation of momentum equation, and the conservation of energy equation form a set of coupled, non-linear partial differential equations–collectively called the Navier-Stokes equations, which describe [fluid flow behavior](https://resources.system-analysis.cadence.com/blog/msa2022-identifying-the-types-and-classifications-of-fluid-flow-regimes).

The complicated or coupled nature of partial differential equations makes solving them analytically difficult. Numerical techniques have been developed to solve such complex differential equations. In physical problems related to fluid flow, heat transfer, and aerodynamics, CFD is prominent in finding accurate solutions. In CFD problem-solving, a physical phenomenon is mathematically modeled and solved using numerical techniques.

## Discretization

Numerical techniques are incorporated in CFD exclusively for solving differential equations. The fundamental principle of including numerical methods for solving partial differential equations is to bring the idea of discretization, one of the key aspects of a numerical solution strategy. Discretization converts the governing equations into a set of simple equations.

There are different methods to discretize the complex governing equations, called discretization methods. A few popular discretization methods utilized in CFD tools are the finite element method, finite volume method, and [finite difference method](https://resources.system-analysis.cadence.com/blog/msa2022-using-the-finite-difference-method-for-the-wave-equation-in-fluid-dynamics).

We will take a deep dive into the finite difference method in the upcoming section.

## The Finite Difference Method

The numerical method of [solving differential equations](https://resources.system-analysis.cadence.com/blog/msa2021-applying-the-finite-difference-method-in-electromagnetics-to-solve-partial-differential-equations) by approximating them with difference equations is called the finite difference method. For the purpose of discretization, the derivatives in the governing equations use the truncated Taylor series expansion in the finite difference method. The implementation of this method is easy and is one of the widely-used approaches to solving partial differential equations numerically.

The finite difference method is based on the secant line approximation of the derivatives. In the place of actual derivatives, the finite difference method uses the difference approximation. Use of the finite difference numerical method results in the generation of a set of algebraic equations that can be solved for the dependent variables. The set of algebraic equations is solved at the discrete grid points in the physical domain under consideration.

To formulate the finite difference scheme, the first step is to discretize the domain into grid points. At each of these grid points, the derivatives are written in the form of differences. The differences can be the central difference, backward difference, or forward difference. The differences relate the values of the variable at each grid point to its neighboring points. After completing this process on all the grid points in the physical domain under consideration, a set of equations are obtained. By numerically solving this set of algebraic equations, the solution for the partial differential equation is determined.

Here is a table that clearly lays out the advantages and disadvantages of the finite difference method.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
| This method is fairly simple. | If not given special care, the physical principle of conservation is not automatically enforced in the finite difference method. |
| Can easily obtain high-order approximations. | This method can be implemented only on simple geometries since the method cannot be implemented with convenience on unstructured grids. |
| Gives higher-order accuracy of the spatial discretization. | The finite difference method requires a structured grid. |

**Advantages**

**Disadvantages**

This method is fairly simple.

If not given special care, the physical principle of conservation is not automatically enforced in the finite difference method.

Can easily obtain high-order approximations.

This method can be implemented only on simple geometries since the method cannot be implemented with convenience on unstructured grids.

Gives higher-order accuracy of the spatial discretization.

The finite difference method requires a structured grid.

The fundamental philosophy of the finite difference method is to replace the derivates in the governing equations with algebraic differences. ## Introduction to Finite Difference Method

The Finite Difference Method (FDM) is a numerical technique used to solve partial differential equations (PDEs) by discretizing the derivatives into finite differences. This method is widely used in various fields, including fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and structural analysis.

### Definition and Basic Concept

The FDM is based on the idea of approximating the derivatives of a function using the values of the function at discrete points. The derivatives are approximated using finite differences, which are calculated as the difference between the function values at two or more adjacent points. The basic concept of FDM can be illustrated using the following example:

Consider a function $f(x)$ and its derivative $f'(x)$. The derivative can be approximated using the forward difference formula:

\[ f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x + \Delta x) - f(x)}{\Delta x} \]

where $\Delta x$ is the grid spacing.

### History and Development

The FDM has its roots in the early 20th century when it was first used to solve PDEs in fluid mechanics and heat transfer. The method gained popularity in the 1960s and 1970s with the advent of computers, which enabled the solution of complex problems using numerical methods. Over the years, the FDM has undergone significant developments, including the introduction of new numerical schemes and grid generation techniques.

### Applications in Fluid Mechanics

The FDM is widely used in fluid mechanics to solve the Navier-Stokes equations, which describe the motion of fluids. The method is used to simulate various fluid dynamics problems, including laminar and turbulent flows, compressible and incompressible flows, and multiphase flows. Some of the key applications of FDM in fluid mechanics include:

## Discretization and Grid Generation

Discretization is the process of dividing the computational domain into a grid of discrete points. The grid generation is a critical step in the FDM, as it affects the accuracy and efficiency of the numerical solution.

### Types of Grids

There are two main types of grids used in FDM: structured and unstructured grids.

The choice of grid type depends on the complexity of the geometry and the desired level of accuracy.

### Grid Generation Techniques

There are several grid generation techniques used in FDM, including:

### Importance of Grid Quality

The quality of the grid has a significant impact on the accuracy and efficiency of the numerical solution. A good grid should have the following characteristics:

A poor-quality grid can lead to inaccurate results, numerical instability, and increased computational time.

## Numerical Schemes and Stability Analysis

Numerical schemes are used to discretize the PDEs and solve the resulting system of equations. The choice of numerical scheme depends on the type of problem being solved and the desired level of accuracy.

### Explicit and Implicit Schemes

There are two main types of numerical schemes used in FDM: explicit and implicit schemes.

### Stability Criteria

The stability of the numerical scheme is critical to obtaining accurate results. The CFL (Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy) condition is a widely used stability criterion for explicit schemes. The CFL condition states that the Courant number, which is defined as:

\[ C = \frac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x} \]

should be less than or equal to 1 for stability.

### Accuracy and Convergence Analysis

The accuracy and convergence of the numerical scheme are critical to obtaining reliable results. The accuracy of the scheme depends on the order of the truncation error, which is a measure of the difference between the exact solution and the numerical solution. The convergence of the scheme depends on the ability of the scheme to capture the important features of the flow.

## Applications in Fluid Mechanics

The FDM is widely used in fluid mechanics to solve a variety of problems, including laminar and turbulent flows, compressible and incompressible flows, and multiphase flows.

### Solving Navier-Stokes Equations

The Navier-Stokes equations are a set of nonlinear PDEs that describe the motion of fluids. The FDM is used to solve these equations by discretizing the derivatives and solving the resulting system of equations.

### Simulating Fluid Flow and Heat Transfer

The FDM is used to simulate fluid flow and heat transfer in complex geometries. The method can be used to analyze the behavior of fluids in various engineering applications, such as pipelines, pumps, and turbines.

### Modeling Complex Fluid Dynamics Problems

The FDM is used to model complex fluid dynamics problems, including turbulent flows, multiphase flows, and flows with complex geometries. The method can be used to analyze the behavior of fluids in various engineering applications and to optimize the design of fluid-based systems.

The following flowchart illustrates the steps involved in using the FDM to solve a fluid dynamics problem:

## Conclusion

The Finite Difference Method is a powerful numerical technique used to solve PDEs in fluid mechanics. The method is widely used to simulate various fluid dynamics problems, including laminar and turbulent flows, compressible and incompressible flows, and multiphase flows. The accuracy and efficiency of the method depend on the quality of the grid, the choice of numerical scheme, and the stability of the scheme.

## FAQ

### What is the Finite Difference Method?

The Finite Difference Method is a numerical technique used to solve partial differential equations by discretizing the derivatives into finite differences.

### What are the advantages of the Finite Difference Method?

The Finite Difference Method is a simple and efficient method for solving PDEs. It is widely used in various fields, including fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and structural analysis.

### What are the limitations of the Finite Difference Method?

The Finite Difference Method can be limited by the quality of the grid, the choice of numerical scheme, and the stability of the scheme. It can also be challenging to apply the method to complex geometries.

### How is the grid generated in the Finite Difference Method?

The grid is generated using various techniques, including algebraic grid generation, elliptic grid generation, and hyperbolic grid generation.

### What is the CFL condition?

The CFL condition is a stability criterion for explicit schemes. It states that the Courant number should be less than or equal to 1 for stability.

# 4. Introduction to Finite-Difference Methods for Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)

Milovan Peric
40 likes
1442 views
7 May 2024
This is the 4th lecture in a series of 20 on Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). The topic is an introduction to Finite-Difference (FD) methods. Described is the derivation of first and second-order approximations of first and second derivative using Taylor series expansion and polynomial fitting. Attention is also paid to the analysis of convergence of approximations on non-uniform grids, both theoretically and by means of numerical experiments.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Finite Difference Methods (FDM) effectively simulate fluid dynamics, solving equations like Navier-Stokes for laminar and turbulent flows, as demonstrated in various engineering scenarios.
Error analysis reveals that FDM accuracy depends on grid quality and numerical scheme stability, with the CFL condition being critical for ensuring accurate and stable solutions.
FDM results are often compared to analytical solutions to validate accuracy, showing that with refined grids, numerical solutions can closely match theoretical predictions.
